preFix.title: Needle-felted puffin
preFix.subtitle: A compact standing bird in merino and wool tops
preFix.excerpt: Build a compact 8 cm standing puffin with a bright orange beak and crisp black-and-white plumage. Three-stage construction works through body shape, surface colour, and facial details. A satisfying intermediate project that fits in an afternoon.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felted bird construction methods widely taught in UK guild workshops. Three-stage body/surface/detail workflow is the standard intermediate approach. Primary references: guild teaching notes and published needle-felting pattern guides; no single public domain text.

content.text: The puffin is one of the most recognisable seabirds in British waters, and its compact, upright shape makes it an ideal subject for needle felting. At 8 cm tall this project is small enough to carry in a box but detailed enough to look convincing on a shelf. You will build the bird in three stages: a dense white egg-shaped core in
merino
, a black upper coat and wing overlay, then the orange beak and red eye ring worked in fine
wool tops
. Each stage builds on the last, so you should complete them in order.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: You will need approximately 50 g of white
merino
roving

for the core, 20 g of black merino roving for the upper coat and wings, and small quantities of orange, red, and yellow wool tops for the beak and eye details. Plastic safety eyes (4 mm) are optional but give a sharper finish than felted eyes.
Method

Stage 1: core body. Pull off a generous handful of white merino roving and roll it firmly between your palms into a tight oval roughly 6 cm long and 4 cm wide. Place on your foam pad and stab all over with a 38-gauge needle, rotating the form as you go. Work inward from the surface so the fibres compact toward the centre. Continue until the oval holds its shape without springing back when squeezed. This is your base.

Stage 1 continued: chest. Add another layer of white roving to the lower two-thirds of the form to build the rounded chest. Felt in firmly so the join between layers disappears. The finished base should be approximately 5 cm tall with a satisfyingly round belly.

Stage 2: head. Roll a smaller oval of white merino, about 3 cm across, and felt it separately until firm. Hold it against the top of the body to check the proportion: the head should be roughly one-third the width of the body. Once satisfied, place the head on top of the body and felt through the join from several angles until the two pieces feel solid.

Stage 2 continued: black upper coat. Lay thin wisps of black merino roving over the top half of the head and the entire back of the body, leaving the face and chest white. Felt in gradually with a 38-gauge needle, building up colour in thin layers rather than one thick one. Thin layers blend more smoothly at the colour boundary. Once the black coat is firm and even, use a 42-gauge needle to refine the white-to-black line across the mid-face.

Stage 2 continued: wings. Pull two thin flat pieces of black merino roving, each roughly 3 cm x 2 cm. Felt each one separately on the foam until they hold a flat paddle shape. Attach each wing to the side of the body by felting along the top edge only, leaving the lower edge free so the wings look naturally dropped against the sides.
Stage 3: beak. Take a small pinch of orange
wool tops

and roll it into a tapered triangle shape between your fingertips. Felt it on the foam until firm. The finished beak should be about 1.5 cm long with a slight downward curve at the tip. Attach to the lower third of the face by felting in at the base with a 42-gauge needle, keeping the tip free.

Stage 3 continued: eye ring and beak detail. Use a very fine wisp of red wool tops to felt a small ring around each eye socket. If using plastic safety eyes, push the post through at this point and press the washer firmly from inside using a blunt darning needle held through the foam. If using felted eyes, felt two small black dots using a 42-gauge needle, then add a tiny white highlight spot to each. Add a thin yellow stripe across the base of the orange beak using yellow wool tops.

Finishing feet: roll two small sausages of orange wool tops, each about 2 cm long. Felt each into a flat wedge and press three short ridges into the top surface with your needle to suggest toes. Attach to the base of the body by felting in firmly. The puffin should now stand upright; if it tips, add a little extra wool to the base to level it.

items.fix: Add more black roving in thin wisps and felt thoroughly with the 38-gauge needle until the white base no longer shows.
items.cause: Layers were too thin or not fully felted in
items.symptom: Black coat showing white fibres through it

items.fix: Push the needle in at 45-degree angles around the full circumference of the join, then repeat from the opposite side.
items.cause: The join was not felted from enough angles
items.symptom: Head wobbles or detaches from body

items.fix: Felt the beak separately until very firm, then reattach using many shallow strokes rather than deep single stabs.
items.cause: The beak was attached before it was dense enough to resist lateral pressure

items.fix: Use a 42-gauge needle to smooth the surface by working in very short, shallow strokes across the high spots.
items.cause: Roving was added in thick chunks
items.symptom: Form feels lumpy rather than smooth

content.text: Finishing and display

Needle-felted pieces do not require washing or blocking. Use a pair of sharp scissors to trim any stray fibres from the surface, cutting close to the felt without cutting into it. A lint roller pressed gently against the finished bird will lift loose surface fibres without disturbing the form.

The puffin can stand on any flat surface. For a more finished display, hot-glue a small piece of driftwood or slate to the base. Keep away from direct sunlight, which will fade undyed natural wools and bright
wool tops
colours over time. The
barbs
on your needle dull with use; if you notice the needle starting to drag or tear fibres rather than sinking smoothly, replace it.

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.6 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
